pattern: Show HN: I built {product} – {metric} in {timeframe}

examples.title: Show HN: 3 years and 1M users later, I just open-sourced my "Internet OS"
Show HN: Twitch Installs Arch Linux – A cooperative text-based horror game
Show HN: HN.Academy – Top online courses recommended by Hacker News users
Show HN: Using stylometry to find HN users with alternate accounts
Show HN: After 2.5 years on my side project, it has hit £500/month revenue

tips: Lead with the outcome, not the product. Concrete numbers + tight timeframe create a 'how is that possible?' reaction that drives clicks. The dash before the metric is load-bearing — it signals a payoff is coming.

pattern: Show HN: I quit my job to build {product}, now making ${revenue}/mo

examples.title: Show HN: My embarrassing personal website from the 90s
Show HN: San Francisco Compute – 512 H100s at <$2/hr for research and startups
Show HN: After 2.5 years on my side project, it has hit £500/month revenue
Show HN: Profit Hunt - Get inspired by profitable online projects
Show HN: $200 Solar Self-Sufficiency  Without Your Landlord Noticing

tips: The quit-my-job framing signals commitment and personal stakes, making readers root for you. It primes the audience for a hero's journey. Revenue figure at the end acts as validation — proof the bet paid off.

pattern: Show HN: {product} – open-source alternative to {competitor}

examples.title: Show HN: I made an open-source laptop from scratch
Show HN: I'm building an open-source Amazon
Show HN: We built an end-to-end encrypted alternative to Google Photos
Show HN: I created an After Effects alternative
Show HN: Monica, an open-source CRM to manage friends and family

tips: Instant positioning: readers already know the competitor, so they immediately understand the value. 'Open-source' signals free + transparent + self-hostable — three things HN loves. Competitor name does the SEO work too.

pattern: Show HN: {product} – I've been using it for {timeframe} and it changed how I {activity}

examples.title: Show HN: Gemini Pro 3 imagines the HN front page 10 years from now
Show HN: Non.io, a Reddit-like platform Ive been working on for the last 4 years
Show HN: 3 years and 1M users later, I just open-sourced my "Internet OS"
Show HN: Imba – I have spent 7 years creating a programming language for the web
Show HN: A friend and I spent 6 years making a simulation game, finally released

tips: Personal long-term use signals the product survived the creator's own 'trough of disillusionment'. It's not a weekend hack — you still use it. Creates authenticity that a pure feature list can't.

pattern: Ask HN: What do you use for {task}?

examples.title: Ask HN: The government of my country blocked VPN access. What should I use?
Ask HN: What are your favorite low-coding apps / tools as a developer?
Ask HN: What overlooked class of tools should a self-taught programmer look into
Ask HN: What is the most useless project you have worked on?
Ask HN: Which tool do you use to create beautiful diagrams?

tips: Simple, open-ended, and invites expertise-sharing — HN's favorite activity. Everyone has an opinion, so engagement is guaranteed. The reader self-selects if they care about the topic. No wrong answer possible.

pattern: Ask HN: How do you handle {common problem} in {context}?

examples.title: Ask HN: Are most of us developers lying about how much work we do?
Ask HN: How much do you make at Amazon? Here is how much I make at Amazon
Ask HN: How do you deal with getting old and feeling lost?
Ask HN: I was hit with a patent troll lawsuit, how do I deal with it?
Ask HN: How do you share/organize knowledge at work and life?

tips: Framing as 'how do you handle' is more engaging than 'what is the best way' — it's personal and practitioner-focused. The context qualifier filters for a specific audience who feel seen and want to share hard-won knowledge.

pattern: Ask HN: Is it just me, or is {industry/tool} getting worse?

examples.title: Ask HN: Am I the longest-serving programmer – 57 years and counting?
Ask HN: Is it just me or GPT-4's quality has significantly deteriorated lately?
Ask HN: Is the market bad, or am I having the worst luck job hunting?
Ask HN: Anyone else burnt out due to extended lockdown and work-from-home?
Ask HN: Azure has run out of compute – anyone else affected?

tips: 'Is it just me' is a permission structure — it lets readers validate a frustration they've been afraid to voice publicly. It's technically a question but functions as a rant catalyst. Converts lurkers into commenters.

pattern: Why I stopped using {popular tool/practice}

examples.title: Why I stopped being a voluntourist
How and why I stopped buying new laptops
Why I stopped travelling to the US and largely stopped doing business in the US
Why I stopped angel investing after 15 years, and what I'm doing instead
Why I stopped reading HN (2010)

tips: Contrarian takes on beloved tools are HN catnip. The pattern works because it promises a reasoned takedown of something the reader probably uses. It respects the reader — 'I have thought carefully about this and changed my mind.' Triggers the 'wait, should I also stop?' reflex.

pattern: {tool/industry/concept} is broken

examples.title: Apollo is dead. Long live Apollo
iPhone Typos? It's Not Just You – The iOS Keyboard Is Broken [video]
TV is broken
Proving that Android’s, Java’s and Python’s sorting algorithm is broken
Goodreads Is Broken

tips: Short, declarative, and aggressive. Works because it names a frustration without hedging. The reader either strongly agrees (and wants to vent) or strongly disagrees (and wants to argue). Both groups click. No neutrals.

pattern: The real reason {widely-believed thing} is {surprising claim}

examples.title: No Copyright Law: The Real Reason for Germany’s Industrial Expansion? (2010)
The real reason we’re upset about Sparrow’s acquisition
The real reason you can't hire developers....
The real reason why Steve Jobs hates Flash
The Real Reason The Poor Go Without Bank Accounts

tips: 'The real reason' implies hidden knowledge and exposes conventional wisdom as naive. It positions the writer as someone with insider information. Readers feel they're about to get the unfiltered truth — a powerful pull in a world full of PR spin.

pattern: {thing everyone obsesses over} doesn't matter
tips: Permission to stop caring about something stressful. Works psychologically as relief — the reader secretly wanted someone to say it. Best when the claim is defensible with nuance, not obviously wrong. The provocative title does the click-work; the content must deliver a real argument.

pattern: I analyzed {large dataset} and found {surprising insight}

examples.title: Show HN: I scraped 3B Goodreads reviews to train a better recommendation model
I analyzed hospital price lists so you didn't have to
I analyzed the chords to 1300 popular songs for patterns. This is what I found.
I analyzed chord progressions in 680k songs
I analyzed 250 SaaS pricing pages

tips: Data + surprising finding = the platonic HN post. The 'I analyzed' framing signals original research, not recycled takes. Dataset size creates authority. 'Found' is more honest than 'proves' — appropriately hedged, which HN respects. Put the finding in the title, not just 'interesting results.'

pattern: {timeframe} of {data/experience}: what I learned

examples.title: Show HN: Plotting 3 years of hourly data in 150ms
Show HN: I’m 16 years old and working on my first startup, a study app
Show HN: I analyzed 6 years of Hacker News data and here's what I found
Show HN: I analyzed 6 years of Hacker News data and here's what I found

tips: Longitudinal personal data is rare and valuable. Time span signals genuine expertise — not a month-old take. 'What I learned' is humble and practitioner-framed rather than prescriptive. Readers trust the source because it's earned through time, not theory.

pattern: We open-sourced {internal tool} – {what it does}

examples.title: Show HN: We open sourced our entire text-to-SQL product
Show HN: We open sourced Lockitron's crowdfunding app
My Startup Failed, So We Open-Sourced the Tech
We've Open-Sourced Everything
We open-sourced 90 node.js modules at Browserling

tips: 'Internal tool' is the magic phrase. It implies the tool was built to solve a real problem, not to be popular — a trustworthiness signal HN weighs heavily. 'Battle-tested at X scale' or 'used in production' preempts the 'is this production-ready?' comment thread.

pattern: Announcing {product} – {one-sentence value prop}

examples.title: Tell HN: Announcing tomhow as a public moderator
OpenAI CEO's Identity Verification Company Announced Fake Bruno Mars Partnership
Announce bad news without lying to your team
WebOS Open-Source plan announced and Enyo Open-Sourced
WhatsApp announced a statically-typed Erlang flavor, open-sourced in November

tips: Clean, professional, and confident. Works best for genuinely novel products — the value prop must be clear in one line. Avoid jargon. If the product requires a paragraph to explain, the HN title will underperform. The dash-separated tagline format mirrors Apple's product naming conventions, which HN finds credible.

pattern: How I {achieved goal} without {expected tool/method}

examples.title: WiFi without internet on a Southwest flight
Shirt Without Stripes
Docker cannot be downloaded without logging into Docker Store
Gaining access to anyones Arc browser without them even visiting a website
California law bans delivery apps from listing a restaurant without an agreement

tips: The 'without X' creates a puzzle: how is that even possible? It implies a clever or non-obvious approach. Best when the excluded tool is one the reader assumes is required. Delivers both curiosity and practical utility — a rare combination.

pattern: How I {built/learned/did} {thing} in {surprisingly short timeframe}

examples.title: Show HN: Portable Secret – How I store my secrets and communicate privately
How I Hacked Hacker News (with arc security advisory)
How I Built a Custom Camper Van (2015)
Operation Luigi: How I hacked my friend without her noticing
How I Built a $5K a Month Side Project

tips: Short timeframe creates aspirational tension — 'could I do this?' Readers arrive to extract the shortcut or method. Works best when the timeframe is genuinely surprising but believable. Unbelievable timeframes backfire with HN's skeptical audience.

pattern: {number} things I wish I knew before {major decision}

examples.title: Bits of advice I wish I had known
Everything I wish I knew when learning C
Tricks I wish I knew when I learned TypeScript
What I wish I knew before moving to San Francisco
What I wish I knew when I became CTO

tips: Regret-shaped wisdom is among the most shared content formats. Readers about to make the same decision click out of self-interest; readers who already made it click for validation. Odd numbers outperform even numbers (perceived as less manufactured). Keep the number under 15 — above that signals padding.

pattern: {number} mistakes I made {building/running} {type of company/project}

examples.title: Ask HN: What mistakes did you make when starting as a consultant/freelancer?
Mistakes I've Made as an Engineering Manager
Some mistakes I made as a new manager
Mistakes I've Made in AWS
SEO mistakes I've made and how I fixed them

tips: Vulnerability plus specificity equals trust. 'Mistakes I made' outperforms 'common mistakes' because it's personal and earned. HN readers are deeply suspicious of advice-giving; framing as personal failure rather than prescriptive wisdom sidesteps that resistance.

pattern: Everyone is wrong about {widely-held belief}

examples.title: Nobody talks about the real reason to use Tabs over Spaces
Nobody talks about the real reason to use tabs over spaces

tips: Maximum controversy, minimum hedging. Works when the post delivers a genuinely well-argued alternative. Fails catastrophically when the contrarian position is weak — HN will dismantle it in comments, which can paradoxically boost visibility but damage credibility.

pattern: Stop {common practice} – here's what to do instead

examples.title: Moderators of HN: please stop changing post titles
Ask HN: Please stop writing tutorials/tech articles on Medium
Tell HN: Please stop offering your services
Ask HN admins: please stop editing submission titles
Things I wish people who write job postings would stop writing

tips: Imperative 'stop' commands attention. The 'here's what to do instead' prevents the post from being pure negativity — it promises a constructive alternative. Works best when the 'instead' is genuinely better, not just trendy. HN commenters will test the recommendation rigorously.

pattern: {product} just hit {milestone} – here's what we learned

examples.title: I created Perfect Wiki and reached $250k in annual revenue without investors
Show HN: After 10 years my side project has hit $8k/mo in revenue
Show HN: Side project passed $2.5k/mo revenue after 2 years (Phoenix and Vue)
WhatsApp Cofounder on How It Reached 1.3B Users Without Losing Its Focus
Blacksky grew to millions of users without spending a dollar

tips: Milestone posts work because they promise both inspiration and tactical lessons. The milestone validates that the writer knows what they're talking about. 'Here's what we learned' signals intellectual honesty — not a victory lap but a debrief. Specificity of milestone matters: rounder numbers feel like marketing, odd numbers feel like real data.

pattern: I replaced {manual workflow} with an LLM and {outcome}

examples.title: I analyzed 180M jobs to see what jobs AI is replacing today
Show HN: I replaced vector databases with Git for AI memory (PoC)
The jobs being replaced by AI – an analysis of 5M freelancing jobs
I replaced 50 lines of code with a single LLM prompt
Ask HN: Have you cancelled any software subscriptions because AI replaced them?

tips: Concrete before/after framing sidesteps the AI hype fatigue. 'I replaced' signals a real commitment — not an experiment. Quantified outcome is essential; vague 'productivity gains' get ignored. Best when the workflow is one readers recognize from their own lives.

pattern: Show HN: I built {product} entirely with {AI tool/LLM} – {surprising result}

examples.title: Show HN: Gemini Pro 3 imagines the HN front page 10 years from now
Show HN: GPT-4-powered web searches for developers
Show HN: Boring Report, a news app that uses AI to desensationalize the news
Show HN: New AI edits images based on text instructions
Show HN: I built an AI that turns GitHub codebases into easy tutorials

tips: Taps into the current 'what is AI capable of?' curiosity loop. 'Entirely with' is a bolder claim than 'with the help of' — and HN rewards boldness when backed by a working demo. The surprising result must be either technically impressive or timeframe-shocking to convert.

pattern: Show HN: {product} – a weekend project that {unexpectedly took off/solved problem}

examples.title: Show HN: Get Paid to Build Your Next Side Project
Show HN: Weekend project, Intro.js
Show HN: After 2.5 years on my side project, it has hit £500/month revenue
Show HN: My self-written hobby OS is finally running on my vintage IBM ThinkPad
Show HN: Exploring HN by mapping and analyzing 40M posts and comments for fun

tips: 'Weekend project' is an underdog framing that HN loves — the small thing that punched above its weight. It signals low ego (not a polished startup pitch) and accidental success (more believable than planned virality). The contrast between effort and outcome is the hook.

pattern: Ask HN: Who is hiring? ({month} {year})

examples.title: Ask HN: Who is hiring right now?
Ask HN: Who is hiring? (November 2016)
Ask HN: Who is hiring? (February 2017)
Ask HN: Who is hiring? (October 2017)
Ask HN: Who is hiring? (July 2015)

tips: Community institution — not replicable as a one-off post, but the monthly format shows how purely functional titles dominate when serving a real recurring need. The date is load-bearing: it makes each post unique and time-relevant. Zero cleverness required when utility is maximal.

pattern: Show HN: After {timeframe} of building, {product} is finally {milestone}

examples.title: Show HN: I took back Video.js after 16 years and we rewrote it to be 88% smaller
Show HN: After 2.5 years on my side project, it has hit £500/month revenue
Show HN: After 10 years my side project has hit $8k/mo in revenue
Show HN: Dillo 3.1.0 released after 9 years
Show HN: Side project passed $2.5k/mo revenue after 2 years (Phoenix and Vue)

tips: Long build times signal obsession and commitment — qualities HN deeply respects. 'Finally' contains emotional weight: relief, perseverance, delayed gratification. This pattern converts better than quick-build stories for products that require trust (e.g., security tools, financial tools, professional software).
